# Membrane-Based Technologies in the Pharmaceutical Industry and Continuous Production of Polymer-Coated Crystals/Particles

### Affiliations

# Membrane-Based Technologies in the Pharmaceutical Industry and Continuous Production of Polymer-Coated Crystals/Particles

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

**Background:**
Membrane technologies are of increasing importance in a variety of separation and purification applications involving liquid phases and gaseous mixtures. Although the most widely used applications at this time are in water treatment including desalination, there are many applications in chemical, food, healthcare, paper and petrochemical industries. This brief review is concerned with existing and emerging applications of various membrane technologies in the pharmaceutical and biopharmaceutical industry.

**Methods:**
The goal of this review article is to identify important membrane processes and techniques which are being used or proposed to be used in the pharmaceutical and biopharmaceutical operations. How novel membrane processes can be useful for delivery of crystalline/particulate drugs is also of interest.

**Results:**
Membrane separation technologies are extensively used in downstream processes for bio-pharmaceutical separation and purification operations via microfiltration, ultrafiltration and diafiltration. Also the new technique of membrane chromatography allows efficient purification of monoclonal antibodies. Membrane filtration techniques of reverse osmosis and nanofiltration are being combined with bioreactors and advanced oxidation processes to treat wastewaters from pharmaceutical plants. Nanofiltration with organic solvent-stable membranes can implement solvent exchange and catalyst recovery during organic solvent-based drug synthesis of pharmaceutical compounds/intermediates. Membranes in the form of hollow fibers can be conveniently used to implement crystallization of pharmaceutical compounds. The novel crystallization methods of solid hollow fiber cooling crystallizer (SHFCC) and porous hollow fiber anti-solvent crystallization (PHFAC) are being developed to provide efficient methods for continuous production of polymer-coated drug crystals in the area of drug delivery.

**Conclusion:**
This brief review provides a general introduction to various applications of membrane technologies in the pharmaceutical/biopharmaceutical industry with special emphasis on novel membrane techniques for pharmaceutical applications. The method of coating a drug particle with a polymer using the SHFCC method is stable and ready for scale-up for operation over an extended period.

**Keywords:**
Membrane separation and purification; drug particles; membrane filtration; polymer coating; precipitation.

# How Does Membrane Separation Work? Here's The Process

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6231288d3b1c4f08aa40a720/1676561688627-60427J653QRVQMFCQGSC/how-does-membrane-separation-work-heres-the-process.jpg)

Membrane separation is a critical part of wastewater treatment in many industrial settings.

Membrane separation is the selective permeation of molecules through a membrane barrier that separates molecules based on their size, charge, and other characteristics.

Today, we’re looking at how membrane separation works, so you can decide if it’s right for your wastewater treatment needs.

## **What Is a Membrane?**

A membrane is a thin, semi-permeable layer that acts as a barrier for separating substances. Membranes are made of varying substances like polymers, ceramics, and metals. A membrane’s structure allows specific molecules to pass through while blocking others.

The pore size of a membrane and the chemical composition of a substance affect its ability to pass through the membrane, which purifies and separates substances based on their size and charge.

## **How Are Membranes Separated?**

Membranes are separated using pressure difference across the membrane. High pressure is applied on one side of the membrane, forcing the smaller molecules through the pores and blocking larger molecules.

The specific type of [membrane separation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/membranes-separation#:~:text=Membrane%20separation%20process%20is%20a,to%20pass%20through%20the%20membrane) process depends on the size of the separated molecules, the desired separation efficiency, and other factors such as temperature, pH levels, and concentration.

## **Types of Membrane Separation**

Membrane separation types include microfiltration, ultrafiltration, nanofiltration, and reverse osmosis. Let’s take a closer look at each kind.

**Microfiltration.** Microfiltration uses a membrane with small pores to filter out particles and microorganisms from a liquid or gas.   
Pressure is applied to force the liquid or gas through the membrane, where particles that are too large to pass through the pores are trapped on the surface of the membrane. This allows for the separation of larger particles, such as bacteria, algae, and suspended solids.

**Ultrafiltration.** Ultrafiltration uses a membrane with smaller pore sizes than microfiltration. Applied pressure to the liquid or gas forces it through the membrane and separates larger molecules like proteins.   
Ultrafiltration can be used to concentrate or purify substances, depending on the specific application.

**Nanofiltration.** Nanofiltration uses a membrane with even smaller pore sizes than ultrafiltration. Pressure is applied to the liquid or gas being filtered, pushing it through the membrane and separating smaller molecules like salts.

Nanofiltration is used for industrial water, removing dissolved salts and minerals that can affect the water’s taste or quality.

It can be used in the food and beverage industry to remove impurities from liquids and produce certain chemicals and pharmaceuticals, where it is necessary to separate specific ions and other small molecules.   
Nanofiltration is known for its high selectivity, making it an effective tool for purifying liquids with a high degree of precision.

**Reverse osmosis.** [Reverse osmosis](https://www.jmarksystems.com/blog/what-is-a-reverse-osmosis-system) uses a semi-permeable membrane with very small pores to separate dissolved substances from water. The process works by applying high pressure to the water, causing it to flow in the reverse direction of the normal flow, a process known as osmosis.   
The pressure forces the water to pass through the membrane, leaving dissolved substances like salts and minerals behind. Reverse osmosis is ideal for treating industrial water, as it can remove dissolved impurities and contaminants, purifying it for its subsequent use.

Reverse osmosis is also used in the food, beverage, and pharmaceutical industries to remove specific ions and small molecules from the water. Reverse osmosis is known for its high efficiency and ability to produce high-purity water.

## **Factors Affecting Membrane Separation**

**Pressure.** Without pressure, membrane separation wouldn’t work. The pressure difference across the membrane determines which substances can get through versus which are blocked.

Generally speaking, the higher the pressure, the more efficient the membrane separation process will be. But that pressure is limited by the membrane material’s strength and durability and the size and characteristics of the substances that are being separated.

A pump can help maintain the right amount of pressure difference across the membrane, ensuring that the separation process is efficient and consistent.

**Concentration.** Concentration impacts how effective separation is, making it crucial to the membrane separation process. The concentration of the substances being separated can influence the size of the pores in the membrane and the pressure required to drive the separation.

When the substance concentration is too high, it increases the pressure required to force it through the pores. This can clog the membrane and decrease the efficiency of the process.

In some cases, a concentration gradient can be established, where the substance’s concentration is higher on one side of the membrane than the other. This helps drive the separation.   
The substance’s concentration can also affect the diffusion rate through the pores, which can impact the overall efficiency of the separation.

**Temperature.** The temperature can affect the physical properties of the substances being separated, including viscosity and solubility, which can, in turn, affect the diffusion rate through the membrane pores.   
In some cases, a temperature increase causes the substances to lose some of their viscosity, making it easier for them to pass through the pores. In other cases, a decrease in temperature can have the same effect. The temperature can also impact how stable the membrane material is, which affects its ability to maintain its structure and function over time.   
Additionally, the temperature can impact the efficiency of the pumping and filtration systems used in membrane separation processes.

**pH levels.** The pH level of the substances being separated can affect the solubility, charge, and stability of the substances, which can, in turn, impact the rate of diffusion through the membrane pores.   
In some cases, a change in pH level can cause a substance to become more or less soluble, affecting its ability to pass through the pores. Additionally, the pH level can affect the membrane material’s stability and structure, impacting its ability to function effectively over time.

Controlling the membrane separation system’s pressure, concentration, temperature, and pH levels will provide high-quality water.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Membrane technologies, like microfiltration and ultrafiltration, streamline drug purification by efficiently removing impurities such as proteins and bacteria, ensuring high-quality products.

Pharmaceutical products demand stringent purity standards; membrane separation achieves this by selectively filtering contaminants, thereby enhancing drug efficacy and compliance with regulatory guidelines.
Reverse osmosis and nanofiltration are crucial for generating high-purity water, essential for manufacturing processes and ensuring the safety and effectiveness of pharmaceutical formulations.
High purity in drugs and water is vital for patient safety, as contamination can lead to adverse effects; hence, membrane technologies play a key role in safeguarding health.
